notes: accept 'a/the friend', 'a/the sword' — no articles in Latin
accept 'holds' for 'habet' only when context demands (here 'has' is standard)
accept 'can see' for 'videt' — both are standard GCSE renderings

set4.translation: The goddesses are holding the sons.
notes: 'deae' here is nom. pl. (goddesses) — NOT 'of the goddess' (dat/gen sg)

set4.latin: tu me vides, sed ego te non video.
set4.translation: You see me, but I do not see you.
notes: 'tu' / 'ego' emphatic — accept plain 'you'/'I' or emphatic 'you yourself'/'I myself'

set4.latin: pater filio persuadet, et filius festinat.
set4.translation: The father persuades his son, and the son hurries.
notes: 'filio' dative — 'persuades the/his son' (NOT 'persuades to the son')

set4.translation: The queen orders the sons to shout.

set4.latin: omnes amici gladios tenent, et deos oppugnant.
set4.translation: All the friends are holding swords, and are attacking the gods.
notes: 'omnes' = all / every — accept 'all the friends' / 'all (of the) friends'

set4.latin: dea subito apparet, et filium ibi videt.
set4.translation: The goddess suddenly appears, and sees her son there.

set4.latin: pater mihi etiam persuadet, et statim festino.
set4.translation: The father even persuades me, and I hurry immediately.

set8.translation: The goddess was seeing her son.
notes: accept 'used to see' / 'saw' / 'kept seeing' / 'was watching her son'
accept 'will be holding' only if context requires — but 'have' is the default

set8.latin: amici in silva apparebant, gladiosque tenebant.
set8.translation: The friends were appearing in the wood, and were holding swords.
The queen will order the sons to hurry home.
notes: 'domum' = home / homewards — accept 'to home' / 'to their home'

set8.latin: vix dea apparebat, sed filii eam videbant.
set8.translation: The goddess was scarcely appearing, but the sons were seeing her.
notes: 'vix' = scarcely / hardly — accept at any natural position

set8.latin: statim igitur omnes amici gladios habebunt, et deos oppugnabunt.
set8.translation: Therefore all the friends will immediately have swords, and will attack the gods.
notes: 'igitur' postpositive (2nd word) — accept 'So, immediately, all...' / 'Therefore, all the friends will immediately...'

set8.latin: iterum dea patri persuadebat, et pater ad silvam festinabat.
set8.translation: The goddess was again persuading the father, and the father was hurrying to the wood.
notes: 'iterum' = again — accept at start or after subject

set8.latin: filii in silva clamabant, et pater non videbat.
set8.translation: The sons were shouting in the wood, and the father was not seeing (them).
notes: accept 'the father could not see (them)' / 'did not see'

set8.latin: filius gladium tenebit, et amicum mox iubebit clamare.
set8.translation: The son will hold a sword, and will soon order his friend to shout.
notes: 'mox' = soon — accept at any natural position

set8.latin: pater filium iubebat e silva festinare, sed filius tamen non apparebat.
set8.translation: The father was ordering his son to hurry out of the wood, but the son however was not appearing.

notes: accept 'kept hold of' — 'held' is the default
accept 'caught sight of' only marginally — 'saw' is the default

set12.latin: deae iterum apparuerunt, et filii clamaverunt.
set12.translation: The goddesses appeared again, and the sons shouted.

set12.latin: pater filio persuaserat, sed filius etiam tum non festinavit.
set12.translation: The father had persuaded his son, but the son still did not hurry at that time.
notes: 'etiam tum' = still then / even at that time — accept 'even then' / 'still at that time'

set12.latin: multi amici gladios habuerant, et deos oppugnaverunt.
set12.translation: Many friends had had swords, and attacked the gods.
notes: 'habuerant' pluperfect — 'had had' sounds odd but is correct; accept 'had owned'

set12.latin: regina filium iusserat clamare, et filius statim clamavit.
set12.translation: The queen had ordered her son to shout, and the son immediately shouted.

set12.latin: illud ego in silva vidi; tam ingens enim erat gladius.
set12.translation: I saw that in the wood; for the sword was so huge.
notes: 'tam...erat' — accept 'was so huge' / 'was so enormous' / 'was so very big'

set12.latin: omnes igitur amici e navi festinaverunt, et in urbem appropinquaverunt.
set12.translation: Therefore all the friends hurried out of the ship, and approached the city.
notes: 'appropinquo + in + acc' or 'appropinquo + ad + acc' both valid — accept 'approached the city' / 'came near to the city'

set12.latin: pater per silvam festinavit, et filium prope insulam vidit.
set12.translation: The father hurried through the wood, and saw his son near the island.
notes: 'prope' + acc = near / close to / next to
accept 'caught sight of'? — no, 'saw' is standard; 'conspexit' = caught sight of

set12.latin: subito illa dea feminae apparuit, et ei persuasit.
set12.translation: Suddenly that goddess appeared to the woman, and persuaded her.
notes: 'feminae' dative — 'appeared TO the woman' (dat with 'appareo' for 'appear to someone')
'ei' dative = to him/her/it — here 'her'; 'persuadeo + dat' — 'persuaded her' (NOT 'to her')
accept 'the son who had been seen shouted'
accept 'the seen son' — stiff but allowed
pluperfect passive — must be 'had been held'
note: 'tenti' is the PPP of 'teneo' — sometimes 'tentus' in texts

set14.translation: The friend, (having been) ordered, immediately hurried.
notes: accept 'the friend who had been ordered hurried immediately'

set14.latin: dea visa apparuit, et omnes clamaverunt.
set14.translation: The goddess, (having been) seen, appeared, and all shouted.
notes: accept 'the goddess who had been seen appeared'

set14.translation: The father loved his sons who had been seen.
notes: 'visos' PPP m.pl.acc — accept 'the seen sons' / 'the sons (having been) seen'

set14.latin: gladius tentus erat, sed amicus non festinavit.
set14.translation: The sword had been held, but the friend did not hurry.

set14.latin: filii iussi erant clamare, et statim clamaverunt.
set14.translation: The sons had been ordered to shout, and immediately shouted.
notes: 'iubeo' passive + inf — 'to have been ordered to Y'

set14.latin: ingens gladius habitus erat ab amico, sed amicus eum non tenuit.
set14.translation: A huge sword had been had by the friend, but the friend did not hold it.
notes: 'ab amico' = by the friend (agent with ab+abl)
'habitus erat' — literally 'had been had' (awkward); accept 'had been owned' / 'had been possessed' for fluency

set14.latin: multae feminae a deis visae erant, sed reginae tristes non apparuerunt.
set14.translation: Many women had been seen by the gods, but the sad queens did not appear.
notes: 'a deis' = by the gods (agent with a/ab+abl)

set14.latin: omnes filii a deis oppugnati erant, et statim ex navi festinaverunt.
set14.translation: All the sons had been attacked by the gods, and immediately hurried out of the ship.

set16.latin: ille pater filium amatum vidit, et eum statim domum iussit festinare.
set16.translation: That father saw his beloved son, and immediately ordered him to hurry home.

set16.latin: omnes amici in silvam festinaverunt, gladiosque tenebant.
set16.translation: All the friends hurried into the wood, and were holding swords.

set16.latin: illa dea filium vix vidit, nam silva ingens erat.
set16.translation: That goddess scarcely saw her son, for the wood was huge.

set16.latin: regina filio persuaserat, sed filius tamen mox non apparuit.
set16.translation: The queen had persuaded her son, but the son however did not soon appear.
notes: 'mox' = soon — accept 'soon did not appear' / 'did not appear soon'

set16.latin: amici naves habuerant, et ad insulam navigaverant.
set16.translation: The friends had had ships, and had sailed to the island.

set16.latin: dea pulchra in silva apparuerat, sed amici eam non viderant.
set16.translation: The beautiful goddess had appeared in the wood, but the friends had not seen her.

set16.latin: tu ingentem gladium tenuisti, et omnes te viderunt.
set16.translation: You held the huge sword, and everyone saw you.

set16.latin: subito multi dei apparuerunt, et omnes perterriti clamaverunt.
set16.translation: Suddenly many gods appeared, and all, terrified, shouted.
notes: accept 'everyone, terrified, shouted' / 'all the terrified (ones) shouted'

set16.latin: pater filios iubebat e silva festinare, sed filii tamen etiam clamabant.
set16.translation: The father was ordering his sons to hurry out of the wood, but the sons however were still shouting.
notes: 'etiam' here = still / also / even — 'still' best fits

set16.latin: pater filios igitur iussit gladios tenere; appropinquabant enim amici.
set16.translation: Therefore the father ordered his sons to hold swords; for the friends were approaching.
